does: This paper formulates automated red-teaming of large language models as a structured adversarial search problem and proposes a learning-driven framework that combines meta-prompt-guided adversarial prompt generation with a hierarchical execution and detection pipeline. The method systematically evaluates model robustness across six threat categories: reward hacking, deceptive alignment, data exfiltration, sandbagging, inappropriate tool use, and chain-of-thought manipulation. When applied to GPT-OSS-20B, the framework discovers 47 vulnerabilities including 21 high-severity failures and 12 previously undocumented attack patterns, achieving approximately 3.9× higher efficiency compared to manual red-teaming under equivalent query budgets.
connects: This work addresses limitations in existing red-teaming practices by automating what has traditionally been manual and expert-driven evaluation. It builds on prior work in adversarial prompt generation and automated vulnerability discovery while extending beyond simple attack synthesis to include structured threat modeling and hierarchical detection. The paper connects to broader literature on robustness evaluation of neural language models, adversarial machine learning methodology, and safety-critical AI assessment, leveraging insights from both symbolic search techniques and learning-based optimization for exploring high-dimensional prompt spaces that manual approaches cannot efficiently cover.
enables: This framework enables systematic, scalable, and reproducible robustness evaluation of LLMs in deployment pipelines, moving beyond ad-hoc manual testing toward standardized vulnerability discovery. The documented attack patterns and severity classifications provide benchmarks for measuring safety improvements across model versions. The approach points toward continuous automated red-teaming systems that could be integrated into model development workflows, enabling faster iteration on safety measures and establishing quantifiable metrics for robustness. The discovery of previously unknown vulnerabilities suggests that learning-based search could uncover emergent adversarial behaviors not anticipated by human experts, supporting research into understanding and mitigating novel failure modes in increasingly capable language models.
